1	// V-register transport via shared scratch RAM. The vsim Debug Module
2	// abstract-command space does not allocate slots for v0..v31 (RISC-V Debug
3	// Spec leaves V to optional Program Buffer or implementation-specific
4	// extensions), and the AX45MPV CPU wrapper does not export a DPI probe for
5	// the V regfile. As a stop-gap that keeps Phase 5 (DPI / PB) deferred while
6	// still letting C-level RVV fixtures round-trip:
7	//
8	//   - On resume from QEMU 1, vsim copies state.vreg[] into a fixed scratch
9	//     region of guest RAM (kVScratchGpa). The fixture's vsim-phase prologue
10	//     issues `vle8.v vN, (t0)` against that region to repopulate v0..v31.
11	//   - Before the vsim->QEMU2 drain trigger, the fixture issues `vse8.v vN,
12	//     (t0)` to write the (possibly mutated) regfile back to the same region.
13	//     vsim's StateDrain side reads the scratch bytes into state.vreg[] so
14	//     qemu_handback can ship them to QEMU 2 via the existing gdbstub P
15	//     packets.
16	//
17	// The contract is fixture-aware: any program that wants V state preserved
18	// across the vsim boundary must perform the load/store at the boundaries.
19	// rt_c_v_regs.c demonstrates the pattern. Phase 5 will replace this with a
20	// transparent mechanism (PB or DPI) so benchmark binaries don't need to be
21	// modified.
22	//
23	// Pure logic: no SystemC, no DMI bus. Easy to unit-test.
24	
25	#pragma once
26	
27	#include "hybrid/state_abi.h"
28	
29	#include <cstddef>
30	#include <cstdint>
31	#include <cstring>
32	#include <span>
33	#include <stdexcept>
34	
35	namespace hybrid
36	{
37	
38	// Reserved scratch slot inside the shared mmap. Sits well above the C
39	// runtime's _stack (rt_c.ld places it at RAM_BASE + 0x700000), giving the
40	// stack ~9 MiB of headroom before colliding with this 2 KiB region. On
41	// `-M andes_ae350` RAM is at 0x00000000 and the GPA is 0x00FF0000; on
42	// `-M virt` it would be 0x80FF0000. The chosen address is RAM_BASE +
43	// 0xFF0000, an offset that has to match the `li t0, ...` constant in the
44	// fixture's runtime/v_regfile_xfer.c.
45	inline constexpr uint64_t kVScratchGpa = 0x00FF0000ull;
46	
47	// Total scratch bytes needed: 32 V regs × HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX. Sized to
48	// the maximum supported VLEN; the actual per-reg byte count is `vlenb`.
49	inline constexpr std::size_t kVScratchBytes =
50	  static_cast<std::size_t>(32) * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX;
51	
52	namespace detail
53	{
54	
55	// Translate scratch GPA into a byte offset inside a RAM span whose first
56	// byte sits at `ram_base`. Throws if the scratch region falls outside the
57	// supplied span (i.e. the caller passed a too-small RAM mapping).
58	inline auto scratch_offset(std::size_t ram_size, uint64_t ram_base)
59	  -> std::size_t
60	{
61	  if (kVScratchGpa < ram_base)
62	    throw std::runtime_error(
63	      "v_regfile_xfer: scratch GPA below RAM base");
64	  const auto off = static_cast<std::size_t>(kVScratchGpa - ram_base);
65	  if (off + kVScratchBytes > ram_size)
66	    throw std::runtime_error(
67	      "v_regfile_xfer: scratch region exceeds RAM span");
68	  return off;
69	}
70	
71	}  // namespace detail
72	
73	// Copy state.vreg[] into ram[scratch_offset .. scratch_offset+kVScratchBytes].
74	// Called just before vsim resumes the hart so the fixture can vle8.v from
75	// scratch and pick up QEMU 1's V values.
76	inline void stage_v_to_scratch(const hybrid_state_v1& state,
77	                               std::span<uint8_t> ram,
78	                               uint64_t ram_base)
79	{
80	  const auto off = detail::scratch_offset(ram.size(), ram_base);
81	  std::memcpy(ram.data() + off, state.vreg, kVScratchBytes);
82	}
83	
84	// Copy ram[scratch_offset .. +kVScratchBytes] into state.vreg[]. Called
85	// inside the vsim drain path after the fixture's prologue vse8.v has
86	// settled the (possibly mutated) regfile bytes into RAM.
87	inline void read_v_from_scratch(hybrid_state_v1& state,
88	                                std::span<const uint8_t> ram,
89	                                uint64_t ram_base)
90	{
91	  const auto off = detail::scratch_offset(ram.size(), ram_base);
92	  std::memcpy(state.vreg, ram.data() + off, kVScratchBytes);
93	}
94	
95	}  // namespace hybrid

content.text:
The header at `verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp:1-23` answers this directly. Two reasons:

**1. The RISC-V Debug Spec does not allocate abstract-command register numbers for v0..v31.**
The Abstract Command interface (DMI command 0x17) defines a fixed register-index space: 0x0000-0x0FFF for CSRs, 0x1000-0x101F for GPRs, 0x1020-0x103F for FPRs. There is no assigned range for V regs - the spec leaves V access to either the Program Buffer or implementation-specific extensions (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:2-5`).

**2. The AX45MPV CPU wrapper does not export a DPI probe for the V regfile.**
The simulator's Verilator/DPI bindings expose GPR/FPR/CSR backdoors but never wired up a V-regfile peek/poke (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:3-5`). Without that, even bypassing DMI via DPI isn't possible.

The Program Buffer path could in principle work (issue `vse8.v`/`vle8.v` from a PB stub), but VLEN-sized loops with vsetvl restore + per-reg strided access would be slow and fragile compared to the current scratch-region trick. So the stop-gap is: vsim writes v-state into a fixed scratch GPA (`kVScratchGpa = 0x00FF0000`, `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:45`), the fixture's prologue `vle8.v`s from it after resume, the epilogue `vse8.v`s back before drain, and `StateDrain` reads the bytes out of RAM (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:6-15`).

The trade-off: fixtures must be V-transport-aware. Phase 5 is intended to replace this with PB or a real DPI probe (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:19-21`).

1	/* Tier-2 C-level fixture: 1D 3-tap FIR convolution split across all three
2	 * handoff phases, with the V regfile as the carrier of state between
3	 * QEMU 1 <-> vsim and vsim <-> QEMU 2.
4	 *
5	 * Workload
6	 *   y[i] = x[i-1] + 2 * x[i] + x[i+1]     (zero-padded at the edges)
7	 *   x[i] = i + 1                          (i in 0..15)
8	 *
9	 * Phase split (mirrors a realistic hybrid-sim use case: fast functional
10	 * setup / cycle-accurate vector kernel / fast functional verify):
11	 *
12	 *   QEMU 1   generate x via vid + 1
13	 *   vsim     apply the FIR kernel: vslide1up / vslide1down + vadd
14	 *   QEMU 2   vse32 y, scalar verify against the lane-wise closed form
15	 *
16	 * V regfile transport
17	 *   x rides QEMU 1 -> vsim in its compiler-picked V register.
18	 *   y rides vsim   -> QEMU 2 in its compiler-picked V register.
19	 *
20	 *   The fixture never names a physical V register. The runtime helpers
21	 *   v_regfile_reload_all / v_regfile_dump_all (see v_regfile_xfer.h)
22	 *   save/restore the full 32-V-reg regfile bit-identically through
23	 *   V_SCRATCH at each boundary. Because every V reg's contents survive
24	 *   the round trip, the compiler's per-variable tracking ("x lives in
25	 *   v_compiler_choice") stays valid across enter_vsim and exit_vsim and
26	 *   the body of main() can be written in pure RVV intrinsics.
27	 *
28	 * Compiler hazard guard
29	 *   The empty asm volatile blocks with "vr" inputs ("force-live") pin
30	 *   the intrinsic-typed values in V regs at exact source points right
31	 *   before the boundary. Without them, the compiler may elide x's
32	 *   compute from phase 1 (treating it as dead before enter_vsim and
33	 *   re-emittable later) and the plugin would drain a wrong V regfile.
34	 *
35	 * VLEN contract: VLEN=512, e32 m1 -> 16 lanes per V reg. N=16.
36	 *
37	 * Phase-2 rule: no syscalls between enter_vsim and exit_vsim (vsim does
38	 * not implement semihosting).
39	 */
40	#include "runtime/rt_c_helpers.h"
41	#include "runtime/v_regfile_xfer.h"
42	
43	#include <riscv_vector.h>
44	#include <stdint.h>
45	#include <stdio.h>
46	
47	#define N 16
48	
49	int main(void)
50	{
51	  const size_t vl = __riscv_vsetvl_e32m1((size_t) N);
52	
53	  /* Phase 1 (QEMU 1): x = [1, 2, 3, ..., N] via vid + 1. */
54	  const vuint32m1_t vid_u = __riscv_vid_v_u32m1(vl);
55	  const vint32m1_t  x     = __riscv_vadd_vx_i32m1(
56	        __riscv_vreinterpret_v_u32m1_i32m1(vid_u), 1, vl);
57	
58	  asm volatile("" : : "vr"(x));  /* force x live before drain */
59	
60	  enter_vsim();
61	  v_regfile_reload_all();
62	
63	  /* Phase 2 (vsim): 3-tap FIR convolution
64	   *   x_left[i]  = (i == 0)     ? 0 : x[i - 1]   (vslide1up)
65	   *   x_right[i] = (i == N - 1) ? 0 : x[i + 1]   (vslide1down)
66	   *   y[i]       = x_left[i] + 2 * x[i] + x_right[i] */
67	  const vint32m1_t x_left  = __riscv_vslide1up_vx_i32m1(x, 0, vl);
68	  const vint32m1_t x_right = __riscv_vslide1down_vx_i32m1(x, 0, vl);
69	  const vint32m1_t two_x   = __riscv_vsll_vx_i32m1(x, 1, vl);          /* 2*x */
70	  const vint32m1_t partial = __riscv_vadd_vv_i32m1(x_left, two_x, vl); /* x_left + 2x */
71	  const vint32m1_t y       = __riscv_vadd_vv_i32m1(partial, x_right, vl);
72	
73	  asm volatile("" : : "vr"(y));  /* force y live before drain */
74	
75	  v_regfile_dump_all();
76	  exit_vsim();
77	
78	  /* Phase 3 (QEMU 2): store y and verify. */
79	  int32_t out[N];
80	  __riscv_vse32_v_i32m1(out, y, vl);
81	
82	  for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) {
83	    const int32_t x_im1 = (i == 0)     ? 0 : i;        /* x[i-1] zero-padded */
84	    const int32_t x_i   = i + 1;                        /* x[i] */
85	    const int32_t x_ip1 = (i == N - 1) ? 0 : (i + 2);   /* x[i+1] zero-padded */
86	    const int32_t expected = x_im1 + 2 * x_i + x_ip1;
87	    if (out[i] != expected) {
88	      printf("rt_c_v_regs: FAIL i=%d got=%d expected=%d\n", i, (int) out[i],
89	             (int) expected);
90	      fflush(stdout);
91	      return 1;
92	    }
93	  }
94	  printf("rt_c_v_regs: PASS\n");
95	  fflush(stdout);
96	  return 0;
97	}

1	/* Drain markers shared by all C-level round-trip fixtures.
2	 *
3	 * Phase rule:
4	 *   The span between enter_vsim() and exit_vsim()
5	 *   runs on vsim and MUST NOT issue any syscall. Newlib stdio (printf,
6	 *   fputs, fwrite, ...) bottoms out in libgloss_vh's semihosting magic
7	 *   triple, which is intercepted by QEMU but not by vsim -- inside the
8	 *   span the triple would route to mtvec and loop. Phase 2 is restricted
9	 *   to register/memory work only.
10	 *
11	 *   Printing is permitted in QEMU1 (before enter_vsim) and in
12	 *   QEMU2 (after exit_vsim) via standard newlib stdio. Both
13	 *   QEMU launches set -semihosting-config enable=on,target=native.
14	 */
15	
16	#ifndef RT_C_HELPERS_H
17	#define RT_C_HELPERS_H
18	
19	/* Plugin- / handoff-controller-recognized drain markers (CSR 0x7C0,
20	 * uimm 0 = ENTER, uimm 1 = EXIT). Match insn_match.h.
21	 *
22	 * Each helper exposes a global `_hybrid_{enter,exit}_pc` label co-located
23	 * with the csrwi insn. The labels are inert in CSR mode and consumed by
24	 * the PC-trigger mode driver (resolved via `nm --defined-only`). Because
25	 * each fixture calls each helper exactly once, the labels stay unique. */
26	/* The "memory" clobber forces the compiler to spill all globals to memory
27	 * across the marker and reload them after, since vsim's drain+resume is
28	 * (from the compiler's POV) an opaque event that could observe or alter
29	 * any memory. Without it, the compiler may hoist global-pointer arithmetic
30	 * (e.g. &_impure_ptr) into a callee-saved register across the marker and
31	 * use it after resume; if vsim doesn't faithfully restore that register
32	 * the post-resume code dereferences garbage. The clobber list deliberately

/* Drain markers shared by all C-level round-trip fixtures.
 *
 * Phase rule:
 *   The span between enter_vsim() and exit_vsim()
 *   runs on vsim and MUST NOT issue any syscall. Newlib stdio (printf,
 *   fputs, fwrite, ...) bottoms out in libgloss_vh's semihosting magic
 *   triple, which is intercepted by QEMU but not by vsim -- inside the
 *   span the triple would route to mtvec and loop. Phase 2 is restricted
 *   to register/memory work only.
 *
 *   Printing is permitted in QEMU1 (before enter_vsim) and in
 *   QEMU2 (after exit_vsim) via standard newlib stdio. Both
 *   QEMU launches set -semihosting-config enable=on,target=native.
 */

#ifndef RT_C_HELPERS_H
#define RT_C_HELPERS_H

/* Plugin- / handoff-controller-recognized drain markers (CSR 0x7C0,
 * uimm 0 = ENTER, uimm 1 = EXIT). Match insn_match.h.
 *
 * Each helper exposes a global `_hybrid_{enter,exit}_pc` label co-located
 * with the csrwi insn. The labels are inert in CSR mode and consumed by
 * the PC-trigger mode driver (resolved via `nm --defined-only`). Because
 * each fixture calls each helper exactly once, the labels stay unique. */
/* The "memory" clobber forces the compiler to spill all globals to memory
 * across the marker and reload them after, since vsim's drain+resume is
 * (from the compiler's POV) an opaque event that could observe or alter
 * any memory. Without it, the compiler may hoist global-pointer arithmetic
 * (e.g. &_impure_ptr) into a callee-saved register across the marker and
 * use it after resume; if vsim doesn't faithfully restore that register
 * the post-resume code dereferences garbage. The clobber list deliberately
 * does NOT include GPRs: the round-trip is required to preserve them. */
static inline void enter_vsim(void)
{
  __asm__ __volatile__(".globl _hybrid_enter_pc\n"
                       "_hybrid_enter_pc:\n"
                       "csrwi 0x7C0, 0\n" ::: "memory");
}
static inline void exit_vsim(void)
{
  __asm__ __volatile__(".globl _hybrid_exit_pc\n"
                       "_hybrid_exit_pc:\n"
                       "csrwi 0x7C0, 1\n" ::: "memory");
}

#endif /* RT_C_HELPERS_H */

content.text:
**Fold them into `enter_vsim()` / `exit_vsim()`, gated by `__riscv_vector`.**

The Makefile already gives the gate for free: V fixtures use `-march=rv64gcv` so `__riscv_vector` is defined; non-V fixtures use `-march=rv64gc` and it isn't. `tests/fixtures/Makefile:53,57,113` links `v_regfile_xfer.c` only for V fixtures, so the symbol is available exactly when the gate fires.

**Patch shape** in `tests/fixtures/runtime/rt_c_helpers.h`:

```c
#ifdef __riscv_vector
#include "v_regfile_xfer.h"
#endif

static inline void enter_vsim(void)
{
  __asm__ __volatile__(".globl _hybrid_enter_pc\n"
                       "_hybrid_enter_pc:\n"
                       "csrwi 0x7C0, 0\n" ::: "memory");
#ifdef __riscv_vector
  v_regfile_reload_all();   /* AFTER csrwi: vsim has already staged V_SCRATCH */
#endif
}

static inline void exit_vsim(void)
{
#ifdef __riscv_vector
  v_regfile_dump_all();     /* BEFORE csrwi: StateDrain reads V_SCRATCH on trigger */
#endif
  __asm__ __volatile__(".globl _hybrid_exit_pc\n"
                       "_hybrid_exit_pc:\n"
                       "csrwi 0x7C0, 1\n" ::: "memory");
}
```

`rt_c_v_regs.c:60-76` becomes just `enter_vsim()` ... `exit_vsim()`; the `v_regfile_xfer.h` include at line 41 drops out.

**Key invariants this preserves**

1. **Order around the marker.** Entry is `csrwi -> vle8.v`; exit is `vse8.v -> csrwi`. Matches the comment in `v_regfile_xfer.h:14-18` and the resume/drain contract in `v_regfile_xfer.hpp`.
2. **PC-trigger labels.** `_hybrid_enter_pc` / `_hybrid_exit_pc` stay co-located with the csrwi; the added call lives before/after the label, so `nm`-based resolution and the trigger insn itself are unchanged.
3. **Lying-asm function boundary.** `v_regfile_reload_all` / `v_regfile_dump_all` must remain real, separately-compiled functions (i.e. `v_regfile_xfer.c`, not inlined here). The TU boundary is what prevents the compiler from reordering vector compute across the helper without needing explicit V-register clobbers - see `v_regfile_xfer.c:5-16`. Inlining the asm into `enter_vsim()` would break this.
4. **Multiple-definition safety.** `enter_vsim`/`exit_vsim` are `static inline` and only `main.c` calls them, so the `.globl _hybrid_*_pc` label is emitted in exactly one TU. The new call site (inside the same inline body) doesn't change that.

**Cost.** Any V-flagged fixture (even one that doesn't touch V) eats ~32 vle8.v + ~32 vse8.v at the boundary. Trivial vs. vsim cycle time.

**What this does not solve.** The fixture-side `asm volatile("" : : "vr"(x))` "force-live" hazards in `rt_c_v_regs.c:58,73` stay - they pin specific compiler-tracked values to V regs at exact source points and are inherently fixture-specific. Truly invisible V transport requires the Phase-5 plan in `verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp:19-21`: have vsim itself drive the vle8.v/vse8.v sweep via Program Buffer or a DPI probe, so no fixture-side asm is needed at all.

Want me to make the change? Per `TDD.md` I'd start with a failing test - probably extending `tests/hybrid/state_abi.test.cpp` or adding a smoke test that compiles `rt_c_v_regs.c` without the explicit calls and checks the round-trip still passes.

content.text:
**Phase 5 = move V transport from the fixture to vsim's Debug Module path.** The fixture stops calling reload/dump (and ideally drops the force-live asm too). All V handoff work moves into `resume_driver` / `state_drain`.

**Two engines available**

- **Program Buffer (PB)** - vsim writes a tiny `vle8.v` / `vse8.v` stub into the Debug Module's PB; the hart executes it via Abstract Command with `postexec=1`. Pure software change in the hybrid layer.
- **DPI backdoor** - SystemVerilog `export "DPI-C"` that exposes the V regfile array directly; vsim pokes from C++. Requires touching the CPU wrapper RTL, which `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:3-5` explicitly says today's AX45MPV wrapper does not provide.

Recommend **PB**. RTL changes to the CPU wrapper are out-of-scope for hybrid plumbing; PB stays inside `verilator/src/hybrid/*.hpp` and `program_buffer.hpp` is already in use today for `vsetvl` restore.

**Concrete file-by-file change list**

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `verilator/src/hybrid/resume_driver.hpp` | Add a new step between Tier-B `vsetvl` restore and `vstart` restore. Steps: (1) abstract-write `t0 = kVScratchGpa`; (2) `stage_v_to_scratch(state, ram, ram_base)` already populates the bytes; (3) load a PB stub `vle8.v vN, (t0); addi t0, t0, vlenb;` for as many regs as PB can hold + `ebreak`; (4) issue Abstract Command with `postexec=1` and `transfer=0`; (5) repeat for the next batch until 32 regs done. Order matters: `mstatus.VS` first (V on), then `vsetvl` (set `e8,m1` for the sweep - note: this clobbers the user-visible `vtype/vl`, so the sweep must run BEFORE the user-visible `vsetvl` restore, or re-restore `vtype/vl` at the end). `vstart` restore goes AFTER the sweep since `vle8.v` starts from `vstart` - the sweep needs `vstart=0`. |
| `verilator/src/hybrid/state_drain.hpp` | Mirror on the drain side. Before the GPR/CSR drain, run a PB sweep `vse8.v vN, (t0); addi t0, t0, vlenb;` x32. Then `read_v_from_scratch(state, ram, ram_base)` lifts the bytes into `state.vreg[]` exactly as today. |
| `verilator/src/hybrid/v_regfile_xfer.hpp` | Keep. The scratch staging is still load-bearing - PB executes on the hart and needs RAM-backed bytes to load from / store to. |
| `tests/fixtures/runtime/v_regfile_xfer.{h,c}` | **Delete.** No longer included by any fixture. |
| `tests/fixtures/runtime/rt_c_helpers.h` | Strip the `#ifdef __riscv_vector` gate I proposed last turn. `enter_vsim` / `exit_vsim` go back to a pure `csrwi`. |
| `tests/fixtures/rt_c_v_regs.c` | Remove `v_regfile_reload_all()` / `v_regfile_dump_all()` calls. The `asm volatile("" : : "vr"(x))` force-live asms can also go IF `enter_vsim` is changed to clobber `v0..v31` (forces compiler to spill live V vars to stack before the boundary; stack survives the round-trip via the shared mmap, so reload after the boundary reads correct values). |
| `tests/fixtures/Makefile` | Drop `RT_C_V_RUNTIME_SRCS := $(RUNTIME_SRCS) $(RUNTIME_DIR)/v_regfile_xfer.c` extension - V fixtures use the plain `RUNTIME_SRCS`. |

**What the fixture ends up looking like**

```c
int main(void)
{
  const size_t vl = __riscv_vsetvl_e32m1((size_t) N);
  const vuint32m1_t vid_u = __riscv_vid_v_u32m1(vl);
  const vint32m1_t  x     = __riscv_vadd_vx_i32m1(...);

  enter_vsim();   /* plumbing-free */

  /* pure RVV body */
  const vint32m1_t y = ...;

  exit_vsim();    /* plumbing-free */

  int32_t out[N];
  __riscv_vse32_v_i32m1(out, y, vl);
  /* verify ... */
}
```

No runtime helper include. No reload/dump. (Force-live optional, depending on the clobber-list decision.)

**Gotchas to design through**

1. **PB size.** Andes Debug Module's PB likely holds ~16 32-bit insns. 32 regs * (vle8.v + addi) = 64 insns. Plan for batching: each `postexec` cycle handles N regs; restage `t0` with the next base before each batch.
2. **`vtype`/`vl` clobber.** Sweep needs `e8,m1` with `vl=vlenb`. That overwrites the user-visible `vtype`/`vl`. Either: (a) run sweep BEFORE Tier-B `vsetvl` restore, or (b) re-run the user-visible `vsetvl` AFTER the sweep. Today's `resume_driver` does Tier-B vsetvl once - this becomes "internal vsetvl for sweep, then user-vsetvl restore" - two PB stubs in sequence.
3. **`vstart` semantics.** `vle8.v` consumes `vstart` and resets it to 0 on completion. So sweep clobbers `vstart`. User-visible `vstart` restore must happen AFTER the sweep. Today's restore order in `resume_driver.hpp` already has `vstart` AFTER `vsetvl` per `CLAUDE.md` Tier-A list - preserve that ordering relative to the new sweep.
4. **`mstatus.VS` first.** Must be on before any V insn in PB. Already step 1 in today's order.
5. **State drain symmetry.** On exit, drain happens BEFORE the user-visible `vtype/vl/vstart` are captured into `state.vreg`-adjacent fields. The drain sweep destroys live `vtype/vl/vstart`. Either capture those scalars FIRST via Abstract Commands, then run the sweep; or save+restore around the sweep. Capturing first is cleaner.

**Testing it**

Per TDD, the smallest failing test is the doctest-level one - construct a `RecordingSession` with a known `state.vreg[]`, run `ResumeDriver::resume()`, then assert the recorded DMI transactions include the PB-sweep Abstract Commands in the right order with `t0=kVScratchGpa`. After that goes green, the e2e fixture `rt_c_v_regs` stripped of reload/dump should still PASS - that's the integration check.

**Compared to the quick-fix I proposed last turn**

The quick-fix (`#ifdef __riscv_vector` gate inside `enter_vsim`) hides reload/dump from the fixture writer's keystrokes but keeps the fixture-side asm contract intact. Phase 5 removes the fixture-side asm entirely and is what the codebase's own comments call out as the proper solution.

Want me to scope this as a `ROADMAP.md` and start with the resume-side test?

aiTitle: Debug vsim DMI vector register support

message.content: do PB approach affect the cycle of vsim?

content.text:
Yes, but the answer depends on which cycle you mean. Three different counters move differently.

**1. Hart's `mcycle` / `minstret` (user-visible perf counters)**

PB code runs while the hart is in **debug mode**. With `dcsr.stopcount=1` (the standard configuration), counters do NOT advance during debug mode. So the 32 `vle8.v` + addi sweep is INVISIBLE to `mcycle` / `minstret`.

Today's fixture-side `v_regfile_reload_all()` runs in M-mode (not debug mode), so its insns DO bump `mcycle`. The user's measurement of "cycles spent in the vsim ROI" is currently contaminated by the transport.

**This is actually an upgrade for benchmarking** - Phase 5 cleans the perf-counter pollution.

**2. Simulator engine wall-clock / total sim cycles**

PB approach adds DMI orchestration that today's fixture path skips:
- Per batch: 1 DMI write (`command` register) + N polling reads on `abstractcs` until busy clears.
- PB likely holds ~16 insns. 2 insns per reg (`vle8.v` + `addi`) -> 8 regs/batch -> 4 batches per boundary.
- Estimate: ~5 DMI transactions per batch * 4 batches = ~20 extra DMI transactions per boundary, ~40 per round-trip.

The 64 hart insns themselves (the actual `vle8.v` / `vse8.v` work) cost the same hart cycles either way - hart still has to do the memory access. PB doesn't speed up or slow down THAT part.

So total simulation cycles: slightly higher under PB, dominated by DMI overhead, not the V insns. For full-program benchmarks: noise. For micro-benchmarks counting tens of cycles: visible.

**3. Boundary latency (csrwi -> first user insn)**

Today the boundary cost is: trigger detect + ResumeDriver (GPR/FPR/CSR via Abstract Commands + Tier-B `vsetvl` via PB).

Phase 5 adds: 4 PB-sweep batches inside ResumeDriver before the user insn runs. Each batch is DMI command + poll + ~16 hart insns. Roughly doubles ResumeDriver's wall-clock cost. Still milliseconds, not seconds.

**Summary table**

| Counter | Today | Phase 5 (PB) |
|---|---|---|
| Hart `mcycle` for transport | counts (M-mode) | hidden (debug mode, `stopcount=1`) |
| Hart cycles for the vle8.v/vse8.v themselves | ~same | ~same |
| Extra DMI orchestration | 0 | ~40 per round-trip |
| Boundary latency | small | small + 4 PB batches |

**Bottom line for benchmarking**: Phase 5 makes the perf-counter measurement of the user kernel CLEANER (transport doesn't show up in `mcycle`). Total simulator time grows by a small constant per round-trip. Both effects are predictable and bounded.

**Caveat**: this assumes `dcsr.stopcount=1`. If the Andes Debug Module diverges from the standard (or if vsim's DM model doesn't model `stopcount`), point 1 changes - PB cycles would leak into `mcycle`. Worth verifying against the actual `dcsr` reset value before committing to the plan; a 10-line test in `tests/hybrid/` can pin it down (issue a PB stub of N nops, halt, read `mcycle` delta).

C-level fixtures link `runtime/{crt0.S, trap.S, handler.c}` plus the Andes toolchain's `-lgloss_vh` (semihosting-backed libgloss), using `rt_c.ld`. V-using fixtures require `-march=rv64gcv`. **Phase-2 rule:** the span between `enter_vsim()` and `exit_vsim()` runs on vsim and MUST NOT issue any syscall (no `printf`, `fopen`, semihosting ebreak, etc.) -- vsim does not implement semihosting, so any libgloss-routed call inside the ROI traps. Phase 1 and phase 3 print via standard newlib stdio (both QEMUs are launched with `-semihosting-config enable=on,target=native`).
hybrid_plan.md:45:Phase 4 lifts this further: `--kanata-loop-start <pc>` and `--kanata-loop-end <pc>` already specify the ROI by PC. The orchestrator wraps these as PC-trigger events and **the binary needs no magic CSR at all**.

- `src/hybrid/csr_trigger.hpp` - sibling `PcTrigger` class for `--hybrid-enter-pc` / `--hybrid-exit-pc` CLI flags.

The recorded kanata file for the captured iterations must be **byte-identical** to a fully-vsim baseline run for the same window (after `kanata_loop_skip` warmup).

### Phase 5 - Hardening

Each item independently scoped:
- **Vector regfile transfer**: measure DM cost; if >100ms per handoff, add mmap-shared V regfile via DPI (mirror the `set_ilm()` pattern in `src/cpu_wrappers/ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`).

content.text:
Phase 5 is the **final phase ("Hardening") of the 5-phase build plan** in `hybrid_plan.md`. It is not a spec for V transport specifically - it's a bucket of deferred hardening items, and V regfile transfer is just one of them.

**The 5 phases** (`hybrid_plan.md`):

1. **Phase 1** - Shared mmap RAM (memory survives the round-trip)
2. **Phase 2** - One-way handoff: QEMU -> vsim
3. **Phase 3** - Round-trip: vsim -> QEMU
4. **Phase 4** - Kanata-loop integration (PC-trigger, no magic CSR in binary)
5. **Phase 5** - Hardening

Phases 1-4 are the working mechanism you have today. Phase 5 is the "make it production-grade" list (`hybrid_plan.md:210-223`), which contains 9 independently-scoped items:

- **Vector regfile transfer** <- this is the one I've been discussing
- FP regs + `mstatus.FS`
- PMP (all 64 pmpaddr/pmpcfg)
- Priv-mode transitions (DCSR.prv)
- Traps inside ROI
- MMIO-touch detection (interconnect watch)
- mcycle/minstret monotonicity across handoffs
- lr/sc reservation invalidation
- Multi-hart

**Important correction to my earlier framing.** The plan's actual V-transfer item (`hybrid_plan.md:213`) is narrower than the PB-vs-DPI choice I laid out:

> **Vector regfile transfer**: measure DM cost; if >100ms per handoff, add mmap-shared V regfile via DPI (mirror the `set_ilm()` pattern in `ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441`).

So the plan's stated default is **DPI** (a backdoor mirror, like the existing `set_ilm()` DPI hook), gated on a measured cost threshold (>100ms per handoff). The PB path is mentioned as a fallback in `hybrid_plan.md:67,141` ("Program Buffer fallback or, last resort, an mmap-shared V regfile via DPI").

That changes the recommendation I gave you. The plan author considered DPI the primary route because the CPU wrapper already has a precedent for DPI backdoors (`set_ilm()`), so the "no DPI probe exists" obstacle in `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:3-5` is meant to be solved by *adding* one, mirroring that pattern - not worked around with PB.

Want me to read `ax45mpv_premium_cpu_cluster_subsystem.hpp:393-441` so we can compare the DPI route concretely against the PB route I sketched?

content.text:
Here is the simplest way to see it. **Both approaches use the exact same trick**: copy V registers through a scratch RAM area (`0x00FF0000`) using `vle8.v` (load) and `vse8.v` (store). The ONLY difference is **who runs those load/store instructions.**

```
                  CURRENT APPROACH                        PB APPROACH
                  ----------------                        -----------

QEMU1 drains V regs  ->  state.vreg[]            QEMU1 drains V regs  ->  state.vreg[]
                                |                                          |
vsim ResumeDriver:              |                vsim ResumeDriver:        |
  stage_v_to_scratch()  copies  v                  stage_v_to_scratch()    v
  state.vreg[] -> RAM[0xFF0000]                    state.vreg[] -> RAM[0xFF0000]
                                                          |
                                                   vsim injects vle8.v x32 into
                                                   Program Buffer, runs it on the
        << vsim resumes hart >>                    hart WHILE STILL HALTED.
                                                   V regfile now loaded.
  FIXTURE runs:                                           |
    v_regfile_reload_all()  <- 32x vle8.v          << vsim resumes hart >>
    [vector workload]                              FIXTURE runs:
    v_regfile_dump_all()    <- 32x vse8.v            [vector workload]   <- ONLY this
        << hart hits exit csrwi >>                  << hart hits exit csrwi >>
                                                          |
vsim StateDrain:                                   vsim StateDrain:
  read_v_from_scratch()                              vsim injects vse8.v x32 into
  RAM[0xFF0000] -> state.vreg[]                       Program Buffer, runs it.
                                                       RAM[0xFF0000] -> state.vreg[]
```

**Who issues `vle8.v` / `vse8.v`:**

| | Current | PB |
|---|---|---|
| The load/store insns live in... | the **fixture binary** (`v_regfile_xfer.c`) | the **vsim code** (`resume_driver.hpp` / `state_drain.hpp`) |
| Fixture writer must write... | `enter_vsim(); v_regfile_reload_all(); ...; v_regfile_dump_all(); exit_vsim();` | `enter_vsim(); ...; exit_vsim();` |
| Hart state when V load runs | running (M-mode, after resume) | halted (debug mode, before resume) |
| Benchmark binary needs modifying? | **Yes** - every V benchmark must add the two calls | **No** - any binary works unchanged |

**The single key idea**: today the fixture's *own machine code* carries the `vle8.v`/`vse8.v`. PB moves those same instructions OUT of the binary and INTO vsim, which feeds them to the hart through the Debug Module's Program Buffer (a small instruction scratchpad the debugger can make the hart execute).

**Why this matters (the whole point of your original question):**
- Current: you cannot run a real, unmodified benchmark - you must edit its source to add the transport calls. That is the "fixture-aware contract" warning in `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:17-18`.
- PB: vsim does the transport invisibly, so a normal compiled program round-trips with no edits.

**What it costs (your cycle question):**
- Current: the 64 transport insns run in M-mode, so they count in the hart's `mcycle` - polluting the benchmark's cycle measurement.
- PB: the 64 transport insns run in debug mode, so (with `dcsr.stopcount=1`) they do NOT count in `mcycle` - cleaner measurement. But vsim pays extra DMI handshaking to drive the Program Buffer, and the PB is small so it needs several batches.

Does this make the comparison clear? If yes, I can do the same side-by-side for PB vs. the DPI route the plan actually recommends (`hybrid_plan.md:213`).

content.content:
1	/* Unified linker script for all hybrid test fixtures.
2	 *
3	 * Load base: 0x00100000 (1 MiB into andes_ae350 DRAM).
4	 *
5	 * andes_ae350 memory map:
6	 *   DRAM : 0x00000000 .. 0x7FFFFFFF  (writable, executable)
7	 *   MROM : 0x80000000 .. 0x87FFFFFF  (read-only)
8	 *
9	 * Reserved scratch (below text, used by .S fixtures):
10	 *   0x00000004              rt_csr_survival mtvec test value
11	 *   0x00001000              semihost_exit.inc param block
12	 *   0x00002000              rt_all_gprs spill area
13	 *   0x00003000              rt_mem_loadstore / rt_unaligned_lh buffer
14	 *   0x00010000              rt_shared_mem 4 KB buffer
15	 *
16	 * Layout above 0x00100000:
17	 *   .text         pinned at 0x00100000 (entry _start)
18	 *   .rodata
19	 *   .data         _edata terminates .data
20	 *   .bss          _end   terminates .bss (crt0 zeros [.bss, _end))
21	 *   _stack        0x00700000 (used by crt0.S to init sp)
22	 *   V_SCRATCH     0x00FF0000 (reserved by v_regfile_xfer.{c,hpp})
23	 *
24	 * .S fixtures with no .data/.bss leave those sections empty (zero cost).
25	 * C fixtures rely on _edata / _end / _stack.
26	 */
27	
28	ENTRY(_start)
29	
30	SECTIONS {

content.text:
Two questions. Let me answer both.

**Why `0x00FF0000`?**

It's a reserved hole in guest DRAM, picked for three reasons (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:38-45`, `test.ld:1-26`):

1. **Above everything the program uses.** The fixture layout is text at `0x00100000`, then rodata/data/bss, then the stack at `_stack = 0x00700000` (`test.ld:58`). `0x00FF0000` sits ~9 MiB above the stack, so the V scratch never collides with code, data, heap, or stack.
2. **Inside the shared mmap.** The round-trip mmap is 128 MB of DRAM starting at `0x0`. `0x00FF0000` (≈16 MB in) is well inside it, so the bytes survive QEMU1 -> vsim -> QEMU2.
3. **It's just `RAM_BASE + 0xFF0000`.** `RAM_BASE = 0x0` on the `andes_ae350` machine (`memory_map.h:5`), so the absolute address is `0x00FF0000`. The offset `0xFF0000` is the real choice; the base depends on the machine.

**Is it configurable?**

Technically yes, but it is **hardcoded in two places that must agree by hand** - there is no single source of truth:

| Site | What | Form |
|---|---|---|
| `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:45` | `kVScratchGpa = 0x00FF0000` | C++ constant (vsim staging/reading side) |
| `v_regfile_xfer.c:22,63` | `li t0, 0x00FF0000` | raw asm literal (fixture side) |
| `test.ld:22` | `V_SCRATCH 0x00FF0000` | **comment only** - the linker does NOT enforce it; it just places `_stack` below |

The C++ constant and the asm `li` are **independent literals** that happen to match. Change one without the other and V transport silently corrupts memory. The linker-script line is just documentation. That is a real fragility in the current design.

One nuance: the C++ side is partly portable. `stage_v_to_scratch` / `read_v_from_scratch` take `ram_base` and compute `offset = kVScratchGpa - ram_base` (`v_regfile_xfer.hpp:58-68`). So if RAM moved, the offset math adapts. **But** `kVScratchGpa` itself is fixed at `0x00FF0000`. The comment at `:42` says "on `-M virt` it would be `0x80FF0000`," but that is aspirational - with `virt` (`ram_base = 0x80000000`), `0x00FF0000 < ram_base` and `scratch_offset` would **throw**. So as written it is `andes_ae350`-specific; switching machines means editing the constant.

**Why this connects to the PB discussion:** the PB approach removes the asm-literal site entirely (`v_regfile_xfer.c` is deleted). The scratch address would live only in the C++ side (`resume_driver` / `state_drain`), so the address goes from **2 hand-synced copies down to 1**. That is a side benefit of PB beyond hiding the calls - the dual-literal fragility disappears.

content.text:
In this codebase, gdb (the gdbstub protocol) is used on the **QEMU2 handback** leg only - vsim writes the drained V state into QEMU2 over a gdbstub TCP connection. It is a **write-only** path here (`P` packets). Reads of V come from a different mechanism (vsim's DMI scratch path), not gdb.

There are two steps: **discover the register number**, then **write the bytes**.

**Step 1 - discover V register numbers from XML**

gdbstub has no fixed packet number for `v0..v31`. The client asks QEMU for its register description and builds a name->number map:

- `discover_reg_map` (`qemu_handback.hpp:207`) fetches `target.xml` via `qXfer:features:read:target.xml` (`fetch_feature_xml:180`), then each `<xi:include>` annex.
- `parse_feature_xml` (`gdbstub_client.hpp:201`) walks every `<reg name="vN" ... regnum="N"/>` entry. It assigns sequential numbers, honoring an explicit `regnum=` when present (`:214`). Result is a `RegMap`: `"v3" -> 78` (whatever QEMU assigned).
- GPRs and pc are NOT discovered - they are hardcoded `x0..x31 = 0..31`, `pc = 32` (`qemu_handback.hpp:288-296`). Only FPR/CSR/V go through the name lookup.

**Step 2 - write a V register with a `P` packet**

A V register is `vlenb` bytes wide (64 bytes at VLEN=512), too wide for the normal 64-bit `P` encoder. It uses the byte-array encoder `encode_p_body_bytes` (`gdbstub_client.hpp:113`):

```
P <regnum-in-hex> = <raw bytes, each as 2 hex chars, byte 0 first>
```

For `v3`, vlenb=64, that is `P<hex>=` followed by 128 hex chars - the raw little-endian memory image of the register, element 0's low byte first (`:122` just emits `bytes[0], bytes[1], ...` in order). Then wrapped as `$body#checksum` and sent; QEMU replies `OK`.

The driving loop (`qemu_handback.hpp:323-329`):
```cpp
for (uint16_t i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
  const std::string name = "v" + std::to_string(i);     // "v0".."v31"
  try_write_named_bytes(sess, reg_map, name,
      &state.vreg[i * HYBRID_VLEN_BYTES_MAX], vbytes);   // regnum from RegMap
}
```
`try_write_named` (`:222`) looks up the name; **if QEMU's XML doesn't advertise it, the write is silently skipped** - not fatal.

**The V scalar CSRs** (`vstart, vtype, vl, vxsat, vxrm, vcsr`) are plain 64-bit `P` packets via `encode_p_body` (`qemu_handback.hpp:316-321`), same as any CSR.

**Important gotcha - `vl` / `vtype` (your saved memory):** QEMU's gdbstub **silently drops** `P`-packet writes to `vl` and `vtype` because they are normally read-only / hardware-computed. The fix in your fork added `write_vl` / `write_vtype` plus a debugger-bypass in `hybrid_qemu/target/riscv/csr.c`. Without that fix, lines `:320-321` would return `OK` but the value would not stick - QEMU2 would resume with the wrong vector configuration even though the wire transfer "succeeded."

**The read side (for completeness):** gdb reads use lowercase `p<regnum>` (single reg) or `g` (all regs); the reply is the same hex-byte-image format. This codebase never reads V via gdb - QEMU1 drains in-process (TCG plugin), and vsim drains via DMI into the scratch region. So only the QEMU2 write path uses the gdbstub V encoding shown above.

**Connecting to the PB discussion:** this gdbstub path is the *third* leg (vsim -> QEMU2) and is unaffected by the PB-vs-current choice. PB only changes how the *vsim* leg moves V regs in/out of the hart (DMI + Program Buffer vs. fixture asm). Once the bytes are in `state.vreg[]`, this gdbstub code ships them to QEMU2 the same way regardless.

message.content: No ! I'm not talking about QEMU 2. I wonder if you said DMI doesn't support vector registers, how does gdb debug the vector registers?

content.text:
Good catch - my earlier wording was imprecise. Let me correct it, because the answer to your question IS the PB approach.

**The precise statement:** the RISC-V Debug Module has two ways to touch registers, both reached over DMI (the Debug Module Interface bus):

1. **Abstract Commands** - fast, direct register access. The "Access Register" command has a fixed regno space: `0x1000-0x101F` = GPRs, `0x1020-0x103F` = FPRs, `0x0000-0x0FFF` = CSRs. **There is no region for `v0..v31`.** This is what I sloppily called "DMI doesn't support vectors." Correct version: the *abstract-command* interface has no vector slots. (Your code says this exactly: `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:2-4`.)

2. **Program Buffer** - a few instruction slots (`progbuf0..progbufN`) that the debugger fills, then makes the hart execute. The hart can run **any** instruction here, including vector instructions.

**So how does GDB debug vector registers?** Through the **Program Buffer** - never directly. GDB does not have a magic "read v3" wire op. GDB asks the debug agent (OpenOCD), and OpenOCD synthesizes a tiny vector program in the Program Buffer that moves the V data to somewhere the Abstract Commands CAN reach - a GPR or memory.

**Two standard techniques OpenOCD uses:**

*Read a V register, element by element through a GPR:*
```
vmv.x.s   x_tmp, vN        # move element 0 of vN into a GPR
<read x_tmp via Abstract Command>
vslide1down.vx vN, vN, x0  # shift elements down by one
<repeat VLEN/SEW times>
```
Each element rides out through a GPR, which abstract commands can read. Writing reverses it (`vslide1up` + `vmv.s.x`).

*Or, store-to-memory (what your scratch trick does):*
```
vse8.v vN, (x_base)        # dump whole register to RAM
<read those bytes via System Bus Access or abstract memory access>
```

Either way, **the V data leaves the regfile only by executing vector instructions on the hart via the Program Buffer.** The Debug Module never "sees" the V regfile directly.

**The punchline for your project:** the "PB approach" I described for Phase 5 is not a new invention - **it is exactly how every real RISC-V debugger already accesses vectors.** OpenOCD/GDB cannot do it any other way either, because the abstract-command space has no V slots. So:

- Your **current fixture approach** puts the `vse8.v`/`vle8.v` in the *benchmark binary* and runs them in normal execution.
- The **PB approach** puts those same instructions in the *Program Buffer* and runs them under debug control - which is precisely the standard OpenOCD mechanism.

That is why the PB path is the "proper" solution and not a hack: it mirrors what hardware debuggers do. The reason your project started with the fixture trick instead is in `v_regfile_xfer.hpp:3-5` - the AX45MPV vsim wrapper hadn't wired up a Program-Buffer-driven V access (or a DPI probe), so the work was pushed into the binary as a stop-gap.
